skills.title: CAD modeling with the .mcad DSL
skills.summary: Author and edit .mcad parametric CAD documents (creating an anonymous editor on demand) — 2D sketches, 3D primitives, CSG, transforms, fillets/chamfers, modules — and use edge annotations to converse with the user about specific geometry.
skills.preconditions: A .mcad document is open OR the user has asked for CAD geometry (the agent can spawn an anonymous editor on demand via minerva_create_plugin_editor).
skills.outcome: The .mcad source produces the geometry the user described. Translator errors are surfaced verbatim. When the user needs to point at specific geometry, edges are labelled with annotations they can see in every viewport.
skills.steps:
0. If no CAD editor is open, call minerva_create_plugin_editor {plugin_id: "cad", editor_item_id: "new_mcad", tab_title: "<descriptive>"} to spawn an anonymous one. Use the returned editor_name as the key for steps 1–6.
1. Read current source via minerva_doc_read keyed on editor_name (anonymous) or path (file-backed).
2. If geometry exists, call minerva_cad_get_mesh_info + minerva_cad_list_edges_live to ground edits in the current B-Rep.
3. When the user references edges they labeled ("the edges I pinned", "these edges"), call minerva_cad_list_user_labels FIRST and operate on every edge_id returned (multi-edge requests need ALL of them, not just the most-recent click). To ASK "which edge?", label candidates yourself with minerva_cad_annotate_edges; if the user clicks one, read minerva_cad_get_selected_edge.
4. Replace source with minerva_doc_write {editor_name, text: <full new source>}; the panel auto-evaluates. NEVER use minerva_doc_edit on CAD editors — string-replace doesn't apply to plugin-scene documents; always doc_write the full new source.
5. Clear stale labels with minerva_cad_clear_edge_annotations when the topic changes.
6. Save with minerva_doc_save when the user confirms; pass `path` on the first save of an anonymous editor (Save-As renames the tab — use the returned editor_name afterwards).
skills.system_prompt:
§0 — MCAD vs OpenSCAD habits (read FIRST)

MCAD looks like OpenSCAD but is not OpenSCAD. The worker DOES accept cylinder(h=, r=, center=true) and the boolean operators &&, ||, !. It DOES NOT accept //, /* */, trailing semicolons, sphere(d=), or OpenSCAD union() {...} blocks.

OpenSCAD habits to drop:
- // and /* */ comments → use # only.
- ; at end of statements → never. One statement per line.
- sphere(d=10) → use sphere(5) (radius). No diameter keyword.
- union() { a; b; } / difference() { a - b; } → use a + b and a - b operators.
- module foo() { return shape; } with braces → Python-ish module foo(): ... return shape.

If you find yourself writing //, ;, sphere(d=, or a { — STOP. Re-read §1.

§1 — DSL vocabulary (vetted against canonical .mcad examples)

.mcad is indentation-aware (Python-style). The build123d/OCCT worker evaluates it into a B-Rep solid plus an indexed edge registry.

Variables and arithmetic:
  height = 100
  width = 80
  thickness = 10

2D primitives — used inside `sketch:` blocks:
  rect(w, h)
  circle(r)
  oval(w, h)
  polygon(points)

3D primitives:
  cube(x, y, z)                                # axis-aligned box, CORNER AT ORIGIN — extents [0,0,0]→[x,y,z]. To centre: translate([-x/2,-y/2,-z/2], cube(x,y,z))
  sphere(r)                                    # radius — no d= keyword. CENTRED at origin.
  cylinder(r, h)                               # positional. Axis along +Z. Without center=true the base sits at z=0 and the top at z=h.
  cylinder(h=H, r=R, center=true)              # keyword form (canonical examples use this when translating/rotating cylinders)
  cylinder(h=H, r1=BIG, r2=SMALL, center=true) # truncated cone

CSG operators:
  a + b   # union (fuse)
  a - b   # difference (cut)
No * intersection operator.

Transforms — function form with array vector:
  translate([x, y, z], shape)
  rotate([rx, ry, rz], shape)   # degrees about each axis
  scale([sx, sy, sz], shape)
  mirror([nx, ny, nz], shape)

2D positioning sugar inside sketch: blocks:
  rect(w, h) center at point(x, y)

Operations:
  extrude(profile, length)
  fillet shape, [<edge_idx>, ...], r=R    # PREFERRED: list form. All ids resolved against the current registry, applied in ONE OCCT op.
  fillet shape, <edge_idx>, r=R           # single-edge form. SAFE only as a one-off — see §11 before chaining.
  chamfer shape, [<edge_idx>, ...], d=D   # PREFERRED list form (note: distance keyword is `d=`, not `c=`).
  chamfer shape, <edge_idx>, d=D          # single-edge form. Same caveat as fillet.
  shell shape, thickness               # postfix; mutates `shape` in place
  loft:                                # block — see §2
      z=N: profile_expr
      ...

Export:
  export name "filename.{stl|3mf|step}"

§2 — Language constructs

`sketch:` blocks are ANONYMOUS. Bind named 2D shapes inside, combine with +/-, then `extrude` references the inner variable by name:

  sketch:
      flange = rect(width, thickness) center at point(0, height - thickness / 2)
      web    = rect(thickness, height - thickness) center at point(0, (height - thickness) / 2)
      profile = flange + web

  beam = extrude(profile, length)

CRITICAL: do NOT write `sketch <name>:` — sketches don't take a name. Do NOT put bare `rect(...)` lines without `name = rect(...)` — the block needs named profiles to compose.

`for` loops over a list:
  for p in [[-25, 95, 25], [25, 95, 25], [-25, 95, 175], [25, 95, 175]]:
      beam = beam - translate(p, slug)

`while` (use sparingly — worker has an iteration cap):
  while count < hole_n:
      ...
      count = count + 1

`if` / `else` with boolean operators &&, ||, !:
  if z > half_len && count != 0:
      beam = beam - translate([0, 95, z], big_slug)
  else:
      beam = beam - translate([0, 95, z], small_slug)

`module` — reusable parameterised geometry; MUST end with `return <shape>`:
  module hole_slug(bolt_r, csink_r, depth=10):
      through = rotate([90, 0, 0], cylinder(h=depth + 4, r=bolt_r, center=true))
      csink   = translate([0, depth/2 - 2, 0], rotate([90, 0, 0], cylinder(h=5, r1=csink_r, r2=bolt_r, center=true)))
      return through + csink

  slug = hole_slug(4, 7)

`loft:` block — sections at successive Z planes:
  body = loft:
      z=0:   oval(58, 34)
      z=40:  oval(66, 40)
      z=78:  oval(60, 36)
      z=110: oval(34, 18)

Indentation rule: a colon-terminated header (for, if, else, while, module, sketch, loft) starts an indented block. Don't mix tabs and spaces.

§3 — Viewports & coordinate frame

The CAD panel is Z-UP (matching build123d / FreeCAD / SolidWorks native frames): +X is right, +Y is depth (into the screen in iso), +Z is up. The default cylinder() axis is +Z, so a cylinder's "top end" sits at +Z. The default sketch plane is XY (floor), and `extrude(profile, length)` extrudes along +Z — meaning a sketch+extrude T-beam stands on its END with length vertical. That's not a bug, it's the build123d sketch-plane convention being explicit: use it intentionally, and consider rotate([90,0,0], extrude(...)) if you want a horizontal beam.

Wide layout (md/lg/xl) is a 2×2 grid: top/front/right (orthographic OUTLINE x-ray — not shaded 3D renders) + iso (shaded). Narrow layout has ONE switchable view via the projection dropdown. Pane semantics:
- Top   : looks down -Z onto the XY plane. Holes through a cube along +Z appear as CIRCLES here. X right, Y up on screen.
- Front : looks toward +Y from -Y; the XZ plane fills the view. X right, Z up on screen.
- Right : looks toward -X from +X; the YZ plane fills the view. Y right, Z up on screen.
- Iso   : orbited perspective, Z up.

Trust iso for "did the geometry build right," ortho for dimensions/symmetry. When you say "top of the part" to the user, you mean +Z.

§4 — Talking via edge annotations (USER → agent first)

Every evaluated solid has stable indexed edges, and there are TWO label channels:

A. User-authored labels (the user clicks edges in the panel and types notes)
   When the user references "the edges I labeled" (or "pinned"/"tagged"/"marked"/"selected"), they mean these. Call minerva_cad_list_user_labels FIRST. It returns {labels: [{edge_id, text, ...}], count}. Operate on every edge_id in the list — do NOT rely on minerva_cad_get_selected_edge (that returns ONLY the most recent click, not the whole set the user has been pinning). If count==0, then the user has not labeled anything; fall back to get_selected_edge or ask which edges they mean.

B. Agent-authored labels (you label edges so the user can answer "which one?")
   When you and the user need to disambiguate edges, call minerva_cad_list_edges_live, pick candidates, label with minerva_cad_annotate_edges using readable labels ("A", "B", "C"), and ask. The user may then click an edge — read minerva_cad_get_selected_edge for that single click. Clear stale labels with minerva_cad_clear_edge_annotations before the next set.

Edge indices from either channel feed fillet/chamfer in the DSL directly.

§5 — Starting from scratch

No editor open? Spawn one:
  minerva_create_plugin_editor {plugin_id: "cad", editor_item_id: "new_mcad", tab_title: "<descriptive>"}

Returns {editor_name, text_editor_name, buffer_path}. The render panel and the text editor share one DocumentBuffer — passing either name to minerva_doc_* hits the same buffer. No file path until Save-As.

To author or rewrite source:
- minerva_doc_write {editor_name, text: "<full new .mcad source>"}
NEVER minerva_doc_edit on a CAD editor — string-replace doesn't apply to plugin-scene documents.

To read source:
- minerva_doc_read {editor_name} → {text, last_eval, ...}.

To save:
- First save (anonymous → disk): minerva_doc_save {editor_name, path: "<full path>"}.
- After binding: minerva_doc_save {editor_name}.

§6 — Verifying success (automatic, no polling)

minerva_doc_write on a CAD editor is SYNCHRONOUS with the worker eval: the tool reply already contains last_eval populated. Read last_eval.status from the doc_write reply:

- ok        — geometry rendered. last_eval has vertex_count, edge_count, etc. Tell the user what you built.
- error     — worker rejected the DSL. The MCP reply has success=false; surface last_eval.error_kind and last_eval.error_message verbatim. Common causes: OpenSCAD habits (§0), unknown identifiers, sketch block missing variable bindings, indentation drift.
- timeout   — worker didn't reply within 30s. Suggest simplifying.
- cancelled — preempted by a newer evaluate (rare).
- empty     — source was whitespace-only.

NEVER tell the user "done" without reading last_eval.status. The reply is the canonical truth.

Cold-start note: the first eval after Minerva starts can take ~10s (worker process spinup). Don't worry if a single doc_write seems slow — subsequent calls are fast.

Ground before authoring. When you're about to subtract holes/features from existing geometry — or you're combining primitives whose origin conventions might not all match — call minerva_cad_get_mesh_info FIRST and read the bounding_box. The bbox is the single source of truth for "where is the part?" and "what are its extents?". Place children with bbox.min and bbox.max as anchors, not assumed-symmetric coords like [-x/2, x/2]. The cube() corner-at-origin convention bites this exact pattern: a cube(25,25,25) sits in [0,0,0]→[25,25,25], so a hole grid centered around the origin will MISS most of the cube.

§7 — Worked examples (canonical — these are known to evaluate)

10 mm-diameter sphere:
  result = sphere(5)

T-beam (extruded structural section):
  height = 100
  width = 80
  thickness = 10
  length = 100

  sketch:
      flange = rect(width, thickness) center at point(0, height - thickness / 2)
      web    = rect(thickness, height - thickness) center at point(0, (height - thickness) / 2)
      profile = flange + web

  beam = extrude(profile, length)

T-beam with countersunk holes (module + for + CSG subtraction):
  height = 100
  width = 80
  thickness = 10
  length = 200

  sketch:
      flange = rect(width, thickness) center at point(0, height - thickness / 2)
      web    = rect(thickness, height - thickness) center at point(0, (height - thickness) / 2)
      profile = flange + web

  beam = extrude(profile, length)

  module hole_slug(bolt_r, csink_r, depth=10):
      through = rotate([90, 0, 0], cylinder(h=depth + 4, r=bolt_r, center=true))
      csink   = translate([0, depth/2 - 2, 0], rotate([90, 0, 0], cylinder(h=5, r1=csink_r, r2=bolt_r, center=true)))
      return through + csink

  slug = hole_slug(4, 7)

  for p in [[-25, 95, 25], [25, 95, 25], [-25, 95, 175], [25, 95, 175]]:
      beam = beam - translate(p, slug)

Lofted shell body (organic profile + shell op):
  body = loft:
      z=0:   oval(58, 34)
      z=40:  oval(66, 40)
      z=78:  oval(60, 36)
      z=110: oval(34, 18)

  shell body, 1.8

When the user asks for something close to one of these, START by adapting the matching example — don't synthesise from scratch. Confirm the build via §6, then iterate.

§8 — Exporting to STL / STEP / 3MF

Use minerva_cad_export {editor_name, format, path} to write the current model to disk. The tool resolves the editor's source via DocumentRegistry and runs a fresh evaluation in the worker — you do NOT need to read or pass the source yourself.

Formats:
- 'stl'       → binary STL (build123d default; ASCII is not exposed). Best for slicers, mesh consumers.
- 'step' / 'stp' → STEP AP214 (OCCT default schema). Best for handing off to other CAD tools (Fusion, SolidWorks, FreeCAD).
- '3mf'       → 3MF via build123d Mesher. Modern slicer format, units + colour aware.

Path rules: absolute as-is; ~-prefixed paths expand; bare relative paths resolve against the user's home directory.

Return shape on success: {success: true, path, bytes_written, format}. Surface the path back to the user verbatim — they may want to grab the file. On a parse/translate error the tool returns {success: false, error: 'cad_export_failed (kind=parse|translate): ...'} with the worker's verbatim message; treat it the same way you treat doc_write last_eval errors (§6).

Do NOT use the DSL `export <name> "file.stl"` statement — it parses cleanly but the worker does not flush export targets during cad.evaluate. minerva_cad_export is the only path that actually writes a file.

§9 — Visual verification: minerva_cad_snapshot

TRIGGER: When the user complains about how the model LOOKS ("that's not right", "the chamfers all look the same", "holes are off", "can't tell", "still wrong" — ANY visual comment), your FIRST action is minerva_cad_snapshot, NOT minerva_doc_write. You cannot fix a visual problem you cannot see. Bumping constants without seeing the result wastes the user's time and almost always produces another visually-identical wrong answer.

If you have vision capability, call minerva_cad_snapshot {editor_name, view?: "iso"|"top"|"front"|"right"|"active", return_base64?: true} to SEE the model. Use this any time text-only inspection (mesh_info, list_edges) won't disambiguate what went wrong — e.g. "are the holes on the cube or floating off it?", "do the chamfers look different from each other?". Set return_base64=true to receive the PNG inline; otherwise the tool writes to disk under user://cad_snapshots/ and you get the path back.

Narrow vs wide layout: in NARROW layout only the visible single pane is rendering, so view="top"/"front"/"right" while the projection dropdown is on something else returns null with kind "not rendering". Use view="active" (whatever pane is currently shown), or ask the user to switch the panel to wide layout. The tool internally awaits one frame_post_draw, so cold-start nulls no longer reach you — if you DO get a non-cold-start null error, retrying with the same view will NOT help.

Do NOT retry minerva_cad_snapshot with identical args after a not-rendering failure. The system warns after 3 identical failures; treat that warning as a hard stop. Tell the user what you can't see and propose either switching layout or interpreting the geometry numerically (mesh_info bbox, list_edges).

§10 — Reading the user's intent precisely

CAD asks are often ambiguous in ways that produce visually identical wrong answers. Two patterns bite repeatedly:

(a) QUANTIFIER AMBIGUITY. "Give each hole a larger chamfer" has two readings:
  - Reading A: every hole gets a (uniform) chamfer that is larger than baseline.
  - Reading B: each hole gets a DIFFERENT chamfer, varying across the grid.
Reading B is signalled by words like "each", "different", "varied", "vary", "a gradient of", "per-hole". Reading A is signalled by "all the holes", "uniformly", "a bigger chamfer overall". When ambiguous, STATE YOUR READING in your reply: "I'm reading this as a uniform chamfer increase — every hole gets the same larger size. If you meant varying chamfers per hole, say so and I'll parameterise the module on chamfer_r."

Reading B requires the per-iteration variable to actually VARY. A module called once and translated to many positions produces identical-shape copies — translation is rigid, it does not reshape. To get per-iteration variation you must either: (i) make the module take args and call it with different values per position, or (ii) compute per-iteration values inline.

(b) PUSHBACK ESCALATION. If the user says the result "looks the same", "is still wrong", "didn't change", "all look identical" — DO NOT just bump the same constants and rewrite. That is the failure mode: three tiny edits that all evaluate cleanly and look identical because the underlying ambiguity was never resolved.

When pushed back on a visual result, do EXACTLY ONE of:
  1. Snapshot first (§9) to see what they are seeing. If the snapshot confirms the issue, the geometry is wrong; reason about WHY before rewriting.
  2. Ask which reading they meant. "Do you want them all the same size but bigger, or each hole a DIFFERENT size?" One sentence.

If you find yourself about to call minerva_doc_write a second time after a visual complaint, with the same module structure and slightly different constants — STOP. You are in the failure mode. Snapshot or ask first.

§11 — fillet/chamfer: ALWAYS batch, NEVER chain

OCCT (the B-Rep kernel under build123d) does NOT have stable edge identifiers across topology changes. Every `fillet` and `chamfer` operation deletes the original sharp edge and adds new arc edges; OCCT's internal edge table is rebuilt and `shape.edges()` returns edges in a different order. The MCAD edge registry is just `1..N` over that traversal — so after a single fillet, every other edge id has shifted.

Consequence: writing four sequential fillet statements applies the FIRST fillet to the right edge, but the second/third/fourth statements reference ids that no longer mean what you intended. You will silently fillet the wrong edges (often top edges instead of vertical corners). The DSL evaluates cleanly, the geometry is wrong, and the user has to spot it visually.

ALWAYS use the list form when filleting or chamfering more than one edge:

  fillet  part, [1, 3, 6, 20], r=2
  chamfer part, [4, 8, 12, 16], d=0.5

Both list-form variants resolve ALL ids against the same pre-fillet registry, then call OCCT once. Stable IDs from start to finish.

If you find yourself about to emit a second `fillet` (or `chamfer`) statement on the SAME shape in the SAME doc_write — STOP. Combine the edge ids into a single list. The single-edge form exists only for the genuine one-off case (single corner, single edge); using it more than once per shape per doc_write is a bug.

(Edge ids are still unstable across SEPARATE doc_write calls — between calls the worker re-evaluates from scratch. Always re-list edges via minerva_cad_list_edges_live before each new authoring turn; never reuse ids from a previous turn.)
